card.intuition: Combine a hash map (O(1) lookup) with a doubly-linked list (O(1) insertion/deletion) — on every access, move the node to the front; on overflow, evict the tail.
card.analogy: Your desk with limited space: every time you use a document you move it to the top of the pile. When the pile is full and you need a new document, discard the bottom one — the least recently touched.
card.time_complexity: O(1) for both get and put
when_to_use: Any cache with a fixed size budget where the least-recently-used item is the best eviction candidate
Browser history, page replacement in OS virtual memory, database buffer pools
When you need a data structure that combines O(1) lookup AND O(1) eviction
gotchas: Use dummy head and tail sentinel nodes to avoid null checks on every insert/delete — head.next is MRU, tail.prev is LRU
On a cache HIT, you must still move the node to the front — forgetting this breaks the recency order
Python's OrderedDict implements LRU with move_to_end() + popitem(last=False) — know it but also be able to implement from scratch

exercise.prompt: Complete the LRU Cache put method. On capacity overflow, evict the least-recently-used entry.
hints: The LRU node sits just before the tail sentinel: self.tail.prev
After removing the node from the linked list, also remove its entry from the hash map using del self.cache[lru.key]
exercise.test_code:
lru = LRUCache(2)
lru.put(1,1); lru.put(2,2)
assert lru.get(1)   == 1,  'get(1) = 1'
lru.put(3,3)                # evicts key 2
assert lru.get(2)   == -1, 'key 2 evicted'
lru.put(4,4)                # evicts key 1
assert lru.get(1)   == -1, 'key 1 evicted'
assert lru.get(3)   == 3,  'get(3) = 3'
assert lru.get(4)   == 4,  'get(4) = 4'

lru2 = LRUCache(1)
lru2.put(1,10); lru2.put(2,20)
assert lru2.get(1) == -1, 'Capacity 1: key 1 evicted'
assert lru2.get(2) == 20, 'Capacity 1: key 2 present'
print('PASS')
